buckets.label: Act 1 · Dublin
buckets.arcVerdict: Act 1 anchor lines hit thick-Dublin target, but the bucket misses the brief at the seams: 78 of 208 lines (mostly short retorts and Marys high-emotion ch07-08 farewell and emigration speeches) sit flat American inside W/E slip contexts where Dublin should surge, so the act reads patchy rather than uniformly thick. Note the measured non-slip baseline is degenerate (only 3 lines, two of them an unscoreable grunt and a child) because in Dublin almost every utterance carries a with-Irish or emotional license, so the 100 median is a small-N artifact; the acts real working register sits near 30-40 with the strong lines reaching 5-20.

buckets.label: Act 2a · Boston (pre-loss)
buckets.arcVerdict: Act2a RE-SCANNED after 17 accepted fixes: the forfeited-opportunity misses (flat-American baseline and the with-Irish/emotional MISSED-SLIPs in ch9-13) now each carry one readable tell, so they read as irish register on-target; the remaining 100s are the intentional American-mimicry beats (the clerk greeting, 'How do you do?') and a couple of bare lines with no natural opening. The act now reads thick-Dublin where it should and slips where it should.

buckets.label: Act 2b · Boston (after)
buckets.arcVerdict: Act2b OVERSHOOTS the 33 target: the mechanical non-slip baseline median is 100 because William's guarded/striving register is now essentially flat American (consistent with the assimilation arc but well above the band ceiling of 40), and the true surviving-dialect floor only shows in a few light tells (25-65); meanwhile the slips behave UNEVENLY, with the W painter-banter in ch16 dipping correctly but 18 emotional grief beats (the children's deathbeds, burying six babies, William and Mary both) left flat American as MISSED-SLIP, so the act fails the brief at its emotional core where Dublin should surge back up.

buckets.label: Act 3 · Minnesota
buckets.arcVerdict: Act3 reads slightly past target on its pure baseline (non-slip lines median Am 100, since William's guarded sales and naturalization talk among non-Irish is fully Americanized), but the act's situational dialect lives in its slips, which mostly behave: grief, home talk and memory correctly surface ye, so, grand, meself, love, a stor and a deathbed prayer at the right beats; the flags are a cluster of MISSED-SLIPs where calm home dialogue with Mary stays flat American, one OVER-SLIP (c'mere to me in a non-emotional beat), and one OVER-IRISH would-ye to a stranger.

buckets.label: Act 4
buckets.arcVerdict: Act 4's non-slip baseline measures at the top of its band (median Am 100, with the many surviving light tells like 'ye'/'grand' sitting in the 55 to 75 range), so the staircase has correctly climbed to mostly American; the strongest emotional and Ireland-memory beats do slip correctly (the 'would ye ever' proposal, 'Ah sure' on Mary, the dropped-g grief cries in ch31), but roughly a dozen of the act's deepest grief and Dublin-memory monologues stay flat American, so the slip series under-fires on exactly the beats that should dip lowest.

buckets.label: Act 5 · Oregon
buckets.arcVerdict: Act5 hits the ~92 target cleanly (baseline median Am=100, all flat-American), and the big set-piece slips behave (the famine eruption 2424, the death monologue 2532b, the remembered Dublin/Boston lines 2615, the church prayer 2618, and the closing tics grand/God bless ye/found ya all surge or survive correctly); the only weak spots are five deep-origin memory beats (2477, 2481b, 2634 x2, 2660) that name Ireland or the dead yet stay flat where a single old tell would let them slip.

buckets.label: Frame · 1925 (Hollis)
buckets.arcVerdict: The Frame baseline sits at the near-American ceiling (median Am 100, above the 92 target band's upper edge), so the staircase top is correct, but the slips badly underperform: only three of the many emotional deep-memory lines (1114 'Aye', 1116 'me children's', 1575 'dyin'') actually dip, while the rawest beats (the Mary confession, the Dublin tenement birth, the bastard-child wound) stay flat American and read as MISSED-SLIP, so the frame currently lacks the downward spikes the brief requires.

lines.speaker: midwife
lines.span: A boy,
lines.note: Two-word functional announcement, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: midwife
lines.span: strong lungs on him.
lines.note: Lungs-on-him is a classic Hiberno possessive idiom, already doing its job.

lines.speaker: midwife
lines.span: Has his father got a name for him at all?
lines.note: Full question flat enough to forfeit a natural Dublin opportunity. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one light tell a Dublin midwife would carry (e.g. an at-all tag), one tell only, no triad.

lines.speaker: the mother
lines.span: William,
lines.note: A bare name; non-lexical, excluded from scoring.

lines.speaker: the mother (William’s birth mother)
lines.span: Sure, I’ve not much for him. I’d hold onto the coat. Does keep me warm.
lines.note: Two tells in an emotional handover line; comfortably thick Dublin.

lines.speaker: Mary Boog
lines.span: We’ll rear him as our own. Not a sinner will hear it from us, ye have my word. God bless ye now.
lines.note: Multiple tells stacked in an emotional vow; thick Dublin baseline.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Ma, what is she doing?”
lines.note: A short child's question identical in any dialect; no dialect signal and too brief to forfeit a real Irish opportunity, so it is excluded from averages.

lines.speaker: Doyle
lines.span: “Are ye thick in the head altogether, boy?”
lines.note: Two-plus tells (ye, altogether as Hiberno intensifier) doing their job at the natural Dublin register.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “I want to learn.”
lines.note: Bare three-word functional statement, identical in any dialect; no forfeited opportunity.

lines.speaker: Doyle
lines.span: “Sure every boy out here is wantin’ something. What makes ye any different at all?”
lines.note: Multiple tells (Sure opener, ye, at all) pitched at the natural thick-Dublin register.

lines.speaker: Doyle
lines.span: “In with ye, boy.”
lines.note: Short line carrying a fronted Hiberno construction plus ye; already irish.

lines.speaker: Doyle
lines.span: “Did ye stitch this together yerself, or was it yer mother gave ye a hand?”
lines.note: Dense tells (ye, yerself, yer, copula-cleft) at natural Dublin register.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Mother doesn’t know how to stitch leather. I did this meself.”
lines.note: One clear tell (meself) carries an otherwise plain line; already doing its job.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “I stitched it together. Found scraps in the trash and laying around. I watched ye and others workin’ on shoes and boots. I put me mind to it, and I’ve slowly learned how to stitch.”
lines.note: A long explanatory monologue with zero tells that forfeits the Irish inflection a thick-Dublin boy would carry; 'trash' also reads American (rubbish/scraps). [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one tell and de-Americanize the vocab: 'you' to 'ye', 'trash' to 'rubbish', or a habitual 'I'd be watchin' ye'; keep a single tell, no triad.

lines.speaker: Doyle
lines.span: “Heh, sure I’ve seen better stitchin’ in Northampton! Look at it! The stitching looks like bird shit! And will it last at all?”
lines.note: Sure opener and at all carry the line at the natural Dublin register.

lines.speaker: Doyle
lines.span: “A man’s toes’d be out the front of this inside a month, so they would.”
lines.note: The 'so they would' emphatic tag is a strong Hiberno syntactic tell at the natural register.

lines.speaker: Doyle
lines.span: “The English?”
lines.note: Two-word echoing question, identical in any dialect; excluded.

lines.speaker: Doyle
lines.span: “They do be makin’ shoes like this. Cheap to make, make it fast, the customer comes back quickly to buy again and again. Us Irish?”
lines.note: Habitual 'do be' is a marquee Hiberno tell opening the line; already irish.

lines.speaker: Doyle
lines.span: “We make proper footwear. The like ye’d be proud of. Our name is on it, you know?”
lines.note: 'The like ye'd be proud of' carries the Hiberno idiom plus ye at the natural register.

lines.speaker: Doyle
lines.span: “I can see ye ain’t goin’ away. But sure I’m a busy man, not much time to teach. You’ll sweep. You’ll carry. You’ll do whatever I tell you and you won’t speak unless spoken to. And I don’t pay, mind.”
lines.note: Ye, sure opener and the final 'mind' tag keep this thick-Dublin despite the plainer middle.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “I’ve no need of pay. I need a trade.”
lines.note: Short clipped declarative pair, identical in any dialect; no genuine forfeited opportunity at this length. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Pa, I was talkin’ with young Jon Murphy. He was after tellin’ me he gets to go across the ocean some day, like. I asked him why. He says it was in his name. He says Murphy meant ‘sea warrior’ and that he was destined to it. I told him I could go over with him. He laughed at me.”
lines.note: The after-perfect and final 'like' carry an otherwise long line into solid Hiberno.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “I remember when sister Mary was after teasin’ me, she told me I was adopted. Then ye and ma told me that I was adopted. Pa, what name was I born into?”
lines.note: After-perfect and ye keep this emotional line firmly irish.

lines.speaker: Pa (James Boog)
lines.span: “You were born into the like of our last name, in a queer aul’ way. C’mere to me, we’ve a power of family over in Scotland. Most of them go by B-o-a-g now. Sure they do be changin’ the spellin’ of it. Me father left Scotland and came over here. He died, God rest him, and meself only a wee lad. Says I to meself, new place, new spellin’. B-o-o-g. Where was I, at all? Oh, the Scots. William, yer father is a distant cousin of mine. He’s a weaver away off in Perthshire. When all of this happened, sure it was plain he hadn’t a want of you. He’s his own wife and a clatter of childer. Your mother is from around here, could be dead by now, she hadn’t a want of you either. We said we’d rear you ourselves. See, your mother had joined a trip to Scotland to get materials to bring back here. It’s hard times altogether fell on her after she came home, and you on the way.”
lines.note: Saturated with Hiberno tells (do be, meself, wee, rear, childer, God rest him); thick-Dublin ceiling.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Does he…”
lines.note: Two-word trailing fragment, identical in any dialect; excluded.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Nevermind, Pa. What does it mean to be a Boog, yer name?”
lines.note: Short question with no dialect signal either way; too brief to forfeit a genuine Irish opportunity. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]

lines.speaker: Pa (James Boog)
lines.span: “Nothing. It means nuttin’. We are the only Boogs in Dublin, perhaps Ireland. Sure, maybe it’ll mean ‘famine survivor’. Don’t be frettin’, William. Quit goin’ on about yer last name.”
lines.note: Th-stopping, Sure opener and 'don't be frettin'' keep this emotional line solidly irish.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Pa, the other childer call me a bastard. They’re tellin’ me nothing good will come from a bastard, that everything I like will leave me.”
lines.note: 'Childer' is a clear lexical Hiberno tell carrying the emotional line.

lines.speaker: Pa (James Boog)
lines.span: “Whisht! Don’t talk that nonsense. They’re just actin’ the maggot. Ignore them and you’ll be on the pig’s back, okay William?”
lines.note: Three marquee Hiberno idioms (Whisht, actin' the maggot, on the pig's back) at the thick-Dublin ceiling.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Aye, pa.”
lines.note: 'Aye' is a single lexical tell on a minimal line; already irish.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Hrmm”
lines.note: Non-lexical grunt, dialect-neutral; excluded.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Hey, what’s the craic?”
lines.note: "What's the craic" is a genuine Hiberno greeting tell.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Uh… hello.”
lines.note: A tongue-tied non-lexical fragment with no dialect signal either way.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “These are yours,”
lines.note: A short functional handover line identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “I don’t think they’d fit me,”
lines.note: A brief deflection too short to forfeit a real Irish opportunity.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “It’s Moran by the way, Mary Moran.”
lines.note: A bare name introduction, neutral and excluded.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Moran? Me pa told me about the Moran family. I bet our parents know each other. Me father is James Boog. I’m William.”
lines.note: Possessive "me pa"/"me father" carries the Hiberno tell across the line.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Boog?”
lines.note: A one-word echo of a surname, neutral.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Aye, has yer family ever mentioned mine?”
lines.note: Two tells, "Aye" opener and "yer," firmly in the Dublin band.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “We only mention what we need to do to survive. Me sister was after bein’ bit by a rat last week. You’d think the famine was over, and us still feelin’ the effects of it.”
lines.note: Thick Hiberno with after-perfect, possessive "me," and fronted clause.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Don’t say such things. We were havin’ such a pleasant walk.”
lines.note: One light g-drop tell keeps it in dialect at its natural pitch.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I read it in the paper. It’s true.”
lines.note: Two short flat assertions with no forfeited inflection opportunity.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Even if it’s true, ye can’t control that, William.”
lines.note: "Ye" carries the Hiberno tell.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “I suppose you’re right. But what are we meant to be doin’ here at all, Mary? Bounce from tenement room to tenement room? When the famine hits, are we all to share a biscuit? Addin’ nothin’ to a name that means nothin’?”
lines.note: "At all" intensifier plus repeated g-drops carry the despairing speech.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Sure, that’s life. That’s the way of it.”
lines.note: Genuine "Sure" opener and the idiom "the way of it."

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Me grandpa Moran once told me to focus on the good things and rely on the church. God has always provided for me, even when times were tough.”
lines.note: Possessive "me grandpa" carries the tell; rest is plain but within band.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Nobody else is comin’ to save you or me,”
lines.note: One g-drop tell at the line's natural bravado pitch.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “In the meantime, I’ll be here with me sisters and brother, helpin’ others when I can. Mendin’ and washin’. It’s just the way of me.”
lines.note: Possessive "me," repeated g-drops, and the "way of me" idiom stack thick.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “And you seem to have found your footin’. Young you are for a bootmaker on his own, but you seem to be findin’ your way. Do you see yourself stayin’ here at all?”
lines.note: Fronted "Young you are," "at all," and g-drops give thick Dublin syntax.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “You see that? I’m goin’ to follow that some day. All the way to wherever it goes.”
lines.note: One g-drop keeps the vow in dialect at its natural pitch.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “It goes down, William.”
lines.note: A short bone-dry retort with no dialect opportunity forfeited.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “It goes down every night and comes back every mornin’. That’s what it does.”
lines.note: One g-drop carries the deflating line.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Aye, but that’s just Dublin’s sun. I want to see where it’s goin’ at night. The one at the edge of the world.”
lines.note: "Aye" opener and a g-drop, two tells in band.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Sure, that makes no sense at all. And what’ll you do when you find it?”
lines.note: Genuine "Sure" opener plus "at all" intensifier.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “I’ll send for you.”
lines.note: A short promise identical in any dialect; too brief to forfeit a tell.

lines.speaker: priest
lines.span: “Do you, William, take Mary to be your lawful wedded wife?”
lines.note: A fixed liturgical marriage formula, not free dialect speech.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “I do.”
lines.note: The fixed vow response, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: priest
lines.span: “And do you, Mary, take William to be your lawful wedded husband?”
lines.note: A fixed liturgical marriage formula, not free dialect speech.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I do.”
lines.note: The fixed vow response, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “This is it, so? Number 7 Aston’s Quay? Our first place together is here?”
lines.note: The tag-question "so?" is a recognizable Hiberno tell already doing its job.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “This is it.”
lines.note: Bare functional confirmation, identical in any dialect; excluded.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “’Tis filthy.”
lines.note: "'Tis" is a clear Hiberno tell at this line's natural Dublin max.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Sure, it’ll clean up.”
lines.note: Genuine "Sure" opener functioning to soften reassurance, already irish.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “The door’s broken.”
lines.note: Flat factual statement with no dialect signal either way; too short to forfeit; excluded.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “I’ll fix it.”
lines.note: Two-word functional reply, identical in any dialect; excluded.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I don’t know. The tenements cost us next to nothin’. It’s what we know. We understand how things work there. Sure, it’s just so busy up here. It’s such a change altogether. It feels as though we don’t belong at all. I just feel...”
lines.note: A long emotional plea that carries multiple Hiberno tells, so it does not forfeit the Dublin opportunity.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Hey!”
lines.note: Non-lexical interjection cry; excluded.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “You’ve got a husband now. Let him lead you about a bit, would ye?”
lines.note: "would ye?" tag question carries the Hiberno tell.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Well, fix the door first. I’ll not have our first customer think we live in a barn.”
lines.note: A two-clause expressive line in flat textbook English that forfeits a natural Dublin inflection. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one light tell, e.g. a fronted or "at all" inflection on the barn clause, keeping a single tell.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “And what harm in a barn at all? In you go, little lamb!”
lines.note: "what harm" plus "at all" are two clear Hiberno idioms.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Enough of that. Besides, sure we can’t go walkin’ in uninvited. Who owns the place?”
lines.note: Genuine "sure" plus the g-dropped "walkin'" carry the line.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Some fella named Joseph. He’s out of town by now. But I already gave him the first month’s rent, so we’ve no choice. We’re staying in this barn.”
lines.note: "fella" is a faint tell but "out of town" reads modern and the bulk runs flat, forfeiting Dublin inflection across several clauses. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Lean the line back toward Dublin by replacing the modern "out of town" feel and adding one tell, keeping a single marker.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “We live upstairs, shop is downstairs. I’ll grab my shop supplies and make me sign and lay out my things down here. I’m a simple man, just throw my stuff upstairs and I can sort it later.”
lines.note: "make me sign" carries one Hiberno possessive tell, just enough for this long line.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I’m well ahead of ye. And I’ll see to those foul garments of yours. They smell of something terrible...”
lines.note: "ye" plus the "something terrible" intensifier construction are both Hiberno tells.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Wait a moment... perhaps ye do live in a barn. That would explain the smell!”
lines.note: "ye" carries the Hiberno tell at the line's natural max.

lines.speaker: Gentleman customer
lines.span: “And these will last through a winter?”
lines.note: Short functional question with no dialect signal either way; excluded.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Sure, those boots’ll see ye through three winters, sir, and by then ye’ll be back. Not because they’re worn out, but because ye’ll be wantin’ a second pair for the summer.”
lines.note: Multiple tells ("Sure", "ye", g-dropped "wantin'") make this thickly Dublin.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “I don’t think I’ve found ye just yet,”
lines.note: "ye" carries the Hiberno tell in this quiet self-address.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Who are ye talkin’ to?”
lines.note: "ye" plus g-dropped "talkin'" are two clear tells.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “I’m not sure.”
lines.note: Bare functional reply, identical in any dialect; excluded.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Sure he’s been doin’ that the whole afternoon. Liftin’ himself up and lookin’ about.”
lines.note: Genuine 'Sure' opener plus g-dropping; thick Dublin already.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “He’s talking to you,”
lines.note: Short functional line, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Maybe he’s askin’ if he’s old enough to help out in me shop.”
lines.note: 'me shop' possessive plus g-drop carries the tell.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “He will, one day.”
lines.note: Bare functional reply, no dialect signal either way.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “He stood up.”
lines.note: Three-word emotional report, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I know. He’s been at it all week, tryin’.”
lines.note: G-drop plus colloquial 'at it' keep it Dublin.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Ye didn’t tell me.”
lines.note: 'Ye' is a clear Hiberno pronoun tell.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I wanted ye to see it for yourself.”
lines.note: 'Ye' carries the line; already doing its job.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Apologies. Me son is unwell.”
lines.note: Flat textbook English ('My son is unwell') forfeits a natural Dublin inflection a shopkeeper would take with a customer. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one light tell, e.g. shift to 'me boy' / 'the wee one' or a 'Sure' soften, keeping it one tell and grief-tight.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “He needs a doctor,”
lines.note: Short urgent line, identical in any dialect; excluded.

lines.speaker: dispensary doctor
lines.span: “Whooping cough. The pertussis. Keep him upright during the fits. Steam from boiled water can help the breathing. Feed him what he’ll take. There’s nothing more to be done, so.”
lines.note: Long clinical monologue from a Dublin doctor stays flat textbook-English and forfeits the inflection a local would carry. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Inflect one clause with a single Dublin tell (e.g. 'There’s nothin’ else to be done' or 'feed him what he’ll take, sure'), one tell only.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “How long?”
lines.note: Two-word functional question, no dialect signal.

lines.speaker: dispensary doctor
lines.span: “Weeks. Sometimes longer. The fever will tell you which way it’s goin’.”
lines.note: G-drop on 'goin'' supplies the Dublin tell.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Not so close. Ye’ll burn him.”
lines.note: 'Ye'll' is a clear pronoun tell under stress.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “He does breathe easier near the steam.”
lines.note: Full clause in a tense exchange with no tell where the surrounding lines all carry one; reads flat for thick-Dublin Act 1. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one tell, e.g. 'He breathes easier near the steam, so he does' or 'near the aul’ steam', a single light marker.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Not so close.”
lines.note: Three-word repeated warning, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I don’t know.”
lines.note: Bare functional reply, no dialect signal.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Mary.”
lines.note: Bare name; excluded from scoring.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I know.”
lines.note: Two-word reply, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: dispensary doctor
lines.span: “How old?”
lines.note: Two-word functional question, no dialect signal.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Eleven months,”
lines.note: A number/age; neutral and excluded.

lines.speaker: carriage driver
lines.span: “Ye can sit up here.”
lines.note: 'Ye' carries the line.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Mary, was this me fault?”
lines.note: Possessive 'me' under grief; properly inflected.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Have ye seen number 6 at all? The one next door?”
lines.note: Two tells ('ye' plus 'at all') make it solidly Dublin.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I’ve seen it.”
lines.note: Short functional reply, no dialect signal.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “The front window is twice the size of ours. You could display six, maybe eight pairs of boots in that window. People walkin’ the bridge would see them before they even reach the door. And the signin’, Mary. There’s proper space above the door for a real sign. Not this little board I’ve got nailed up. A proper painted sign that says somethin’.”
lines.note: Long pitch but g-drops thread through; carries the Dublin sound at its natural max.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “We’re barely the two years in the place, William.”
lines.note: Full objecting clause with no tell amid thick-Dublin banter forfeits an easy inflection. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one light tell, e.g. 'Sure we’re barely two years in the place' or 'in the place at all'.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “A year and a half. And the storefront was the problem. I’m losin’ customers because they walk right past number 7 without a second look. Number 6 has a better front, a bigger window, and the landlord saw to the upkeep. It’s only the one door over, Mary. We’d barely a thing to be carryin’.”
lines.note: G-drops plus the 'to be carryin'' construction are clear Hiberno syntax.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “It’s next door, William.”
lines.note: Short flat retort, identical in any dialect; excluded.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Exactly! Sure we could practically throw our things from one door to the other.”
lines.note: Expressive joke line that forfeits the Dublin lilt the surrounding banter carries. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one tell, e.g. 'Sure we could practically throw our things from the one door to the other.'

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Don’t you dare throw my curtains.”
lines.note: Idiomatic but dialect-neutral scold; works in any register.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “I’ll cradle them grand, so I will. Both hands.”
lines.note: 'grand' plus the 'so I will' tag are two strong Dublin tells.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I wouldn’t mind leavin’ this room either. Every time I look at that aul’ corner...”
lines.note: 'aul'' plus g-drop under grief; properly inflected.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “A fresh start, then. Sure, right next door.”
lines.note: Genuine soft 'Sure' carries it tenderly.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “I stepped on Austin’s hand this mornin’.”
lines.note: G-drop supplies the tell.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I know. Heard him from downstairs.”
lines.note: Clipped reply with no dialect signal either direction.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “There’s no room at all, Mary. I can’t get from the bed to the door without steppin’ over two childer. Austin sleeps with his arms out like he thinks he’s a bird. And the laundry. I’ve got nappies dryin’ over me workbench. A customer watched me pull a nappy off a pair of boots yesterday.”
lines.note: 'at all', 'childer' and possessive 'me' stack thick Dublin tells.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Would ye tell me where else I’m supposed to dry them? The room is what it is.”
lines.note: 'Would ye' carries the line.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “That’s exactly my point. The room is what it is, and what it is, is too small for five of us. Six, countin’ the next one.”
lines.note: G-drop holds the tell at the line's natural level.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “The next one?”
lines.note: Echo question, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “I’m just sayin’. At the rate we’re goin’.”
lines.note: Double g-drop gives the Dublin sound.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “At the rate you’re goin’. Don’t put that on both of us.”
lines.note: G-drop carries it at its natural level.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Oh no. No. William.”
lines.note: Non-lexical refusal and a name; excluded.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Number 13.”
lines.note: A number/label; neutral and excluded.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Ye cannot be serious. Are ye gone soft in the head?”
lines.note: 'Ye' twice plus the idiom make it solidly Dublin.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “C’mere to me.” He leaned forward, animated, his hands already describin’ the shape of the thing he had been buildin’ in his head for weeks. “Number 13 is the corner unit, right at the foot of the O’Connell Bridge. Every single person who crosses that bridge walks past number 13. I’ve counted them, Mary. I stood outside and counted. Between two and four o’clock, more people pass that corner than pass this shop in an entire day.”
lines.note: The spoken dialogue itself (narration g-drops aside) is a long flat pitch with no Hiberno tell, forfeiting the lilt his other speeches carry. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one tell to the speech (not the narration), e.g. 'Every single soul who crosses that bridge' or 'I’ve counted them, sure', one marker only.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “You’ve been standin’ on the street countin’ people.”
lines.note: Double g-drop carries the Dublin sound.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “For three weeks.”
lines.note: Short functional reply with a number; excluded.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Three weeks.”
lines.note: Echoed number, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “And the rooms above are bigger. Proper rooms, Mary. Space ye’d have to breathe. The childer would have space to crawl around without endin’ up under me workbench. And the rent, aye, it’s more, but the foot traffic will more than cover it. Number 13 is where the money is. Sure, I can feel it.”
lines.note: Stacked tells (ye'd, childer, aye, me, Sure) make this thick Dublin.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “This will be our third home on Aston’s Quay. People will think we’re runnin’ from somethin’.”
lines.note: G-drops supply the tell.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “We’re not runnin’ from anythin’. We’re runnin’ toward the corner with all the money.”
lines.note: G-drops carry it at its natural level.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Ye’re impossible. You know I don’t like change. I don’t like this.”
lines.note: 'Ye're' carries the line.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “I know. Sure, didn’t ye marry me anyway?”
lines.note: 'Sure', 'ye' and the fronted 'didn’t ye' tag stack thick Dublin.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Fine,” Mary said. “But you’re carryin’ every last thing. And this time, William Boog, if ye so much as look at another shopfront on this quay, I will nail your boots to the floor.”
lines.note: 'ye' plus g-drop keep it Dublin through the threat.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “You won’t regret it,”
lines.note: Short tender line, identical in any dialect; excluded.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I already regret it,”
lines.note: Short answering line, no dialect signal; excluded.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Yer pa has us dragged up and down this quay like a man lookin’ for his hat,”
lines.note: Carries 'Yer' plus the eye-dialect 'lookin'' and a homely Dublin simile.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “And d’ye know what? I think he’s finally found it.”
lines.note: Flat standard English with no tell where a Dublin mother would inflect the fond aside. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one light tell, e.g. soften with 'sure' or 'd'ye know what at all', keeping it tender not staged.

lines.speaker: Austin
lines.span: “She kicked me!”
lines.note: Two-word startled cry, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “That’s a Boog for ye. Comes out fightin’!”
lines.note: 'for ye' plus dropped subject and eye-dialect carry the brogue.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Bucko, she gets that from me,”
lines.note: 'Bucko' is a recognizable Hiberno address term.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “What are you writin’? A letter?”
lines.note: Single eye-dialect tell on an otherwise plain question; at its natural max.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “An advertisement. For the paper.”
lines.note: Bare functional reply, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “An advertisement. In the actual newspaper, is it?”
lines.note: Incredulous repeat long enough to forfeit an Irish inflection a Dubliner would take. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one tell of disbelief, e.g. open with 'Sure' or end with 'at all', not staged.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “The Irish Times. Sure, every man in Dublin reads it. Every man in Dublin needs boots.”
lines.note: 'Sure' functions as a genuine Hiberno opener asserting shared truth.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “And how much does an advertisement in the Irish Times cost at all?”
lines.note: A full pressing question with no tell where a Dublin wife would inflect. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one light tell, e.g. 'And how much, in the name of God' or 'at all', kept plain.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “It’s an investment.”
lines.note: Three-word functional reply, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “That’s not a number, William.”
lines.note: Short retort that reads naturally in any dialect.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “‘13 Aston’s Quay. Extraordinary Reduction in the Prices of Ladies’ and Gentlemen’s Dress and Walking Boots.’”
lines.note: Read-aloud printed advertisement copy, not speech in a dialect.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “‘We keep nothing but our own manufacture, as we don’t encourage Northampton trash.’”
lines.note: Quoted printed advertisement copy, excluded from dialect scoring.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Northampton trash?”
lines.note: Two-word echo of the printed phrase, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Northampton trash! That’s what it is and that’s what I’ll call it. English factory boots fallin’ apart after a fortnight. I won’t have it in me shop and I won’t have it on this quay.”
lines.note: 'me shop' plus 'fallin'' and the indignant rhythm make this thickly Dublin.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “You’re puttin’ that in the paper? ‘Northampton trash’?”
lines.note: Single eye-dialect tell on a plain question; at its natural Dublin max.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “In bold, sure, if they’ll let me. ‘William Boog, 13 Aston’s Quay. Near O’Connell Bridge.’”
lines.note: 'sure' as an emphatic tag carries the brogue before the read-aloud signature.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “You’re goin’ to start a war with every English bootmaker in Dublin.”
lines.note: Single eye-dialect tell; otherwise plain warning at its natural max.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Grand. Let them come. I’ll make them a proper pair of boots and they’ll never go back.”
lines.note: 'Grand' as an affirming opener is a recognizable Hiberno tell.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Ye’re mad, ye aul’ fool. Put it in the paper.”
lines.note: Three tells stacked: 'ye're', 'ye', 'aul''; thick and fond.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Ye’re out of words, and I’m out of energy,”
lines.note: 'Ye're' carries the line; the rest is plain.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Me body needs to rest. I need to rest.”
lines.note: 'Me body' is the Hiberno possessive; one clear tell.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Boog family, ye’ll have to put up with foul-smelling garments for a while.”
lines.note: A whole jesting address with no tell and 'foul-smelling garments' reading textbook-flat. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one tell and homelier word, e.g. 'yiz'll have to put up with' and 'stink' rather than 'foul-smelling garments'.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “How long has he been like this, the poor child?”
lines.note: The tagged-on 'the poor child' is a Hiberno right-dislocation under alarm.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Since late mornin’. He’ll not take a drop of water. I’ve tried, so I have.”
lines.note: 'He'll not' and the emphatic 'so I have' tag are strongly Hiberno.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Mary, the doctor is here.”
lines.note: Bare functional announcement, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: dispensary doctor
lines.span: “Mr. Boog, I’m sorry for your trouble. Diphtheria is ruthless. I have to see it far too often. It’s very common unfortunately.”
markers: sorry for your trouble (set Hiberno condolence)
lines.note: 'I'm sorry for your trouble' is the fixed Irish condolence formula.

lines.speaker: dockworker
lines.span: “Did ye hear about O’Malley?”
lines.note: Tag-question 'right?' is distinctly American where a Dubliner would say 'so' or 'at all'. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Replace the American 'right?' tag with a Hiberno one, e.g. 'so?' or 'will ye have heard about O'Malley?'

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Which one? Sure there’s more O’Malleys than rats in the tenements.”
lines.note: 'Sure there's more...' uses the Hiberno opener and singular concord.

lines.speaker: dockworker
lines.span: “Patrick! Ye know the man, walks with a gimp, patchy beard, Sean’s brother.”
lines.note: 'Ye know the man' plus the dropped relative pronoun reads Dublin.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “What about him... is he after bein’ caught stealin’?”
lines.note: The 'after being caught' construction is a hallmark Hiberno after-perfect.

lines.speaker: dockworker
lines.span: “There’s more to it than that. The bold bastard stole a snuff box, wantin’ to play the fine gentleman with a pipe like the rich folk. Mayor Bulfin set his down in Stephen’s Green and turned his back for a minute, and didn’t O’Malley lift it!”
lines.note: 'Bold' in the Irish sense and the 'and didn't he...' narrative fronting are richly Hiberno.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “He’s a dead man’,”
lines.note: Short figurative verdict, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “He’ll never find work again on this quay or any other at all. Poor bastard. Ha!”
lines.note: Expressive enough to forfeit a tell; reads flat for thick-Dublin banter. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one light tell, e.g. 'sure he'll never find work' or 'the poor divil', kept to one.

lines.speaker: second laborer
lines.span: “No difference here anyway,”
lines.note: Short functional remark, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: second laborer
lines.span: “Sure the work around the docks is dryin’ up like a tinker’s well.”
lines.note: 'Sure' opener plus the Irish 'tinker's well' simile; thick.

lines.speaker: dockworker
lines.span: “Me cousin wrote from New York. Says there’s work for any man with two hands and a strong back.”
lines.note: 'Me cousin' plus dropped subject 'Says' carry the brogue.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Aye, and half of them end up in the same class of slum over there,”
lines.note: 'Aye' is a clear Hiberno affirmative opener.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “only with Yankee accents and bigger rats.”
lines.note: Continuation punchline fragment with no dialect signal either way.

lines.speaker: dockworker
lines.span: “But it’s different, isn’t it? Over there they don’t care where ye’re from. Ye work, ye eat. Ye don’t work, ye starve. Same as here, except there’s more to eat. No famines like the ones we’ve known, God between us and all harm.”
markers: God between us and all harm (blessing)
lines.note: Repeated 'ye' plus the set blessing 'God between us and all harm' make this thickly Dublin.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “No famines? How much for the passage?”
lines.note: Short clipped questions, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: dockworker
lines.span: “Would ye look at Mr. Fancy Boots here, wantin’ to go to the United States!”
lines.note: 'Would ye look at' is a stock Hiberno exclamation; thick with 'ye' and 'wantin''.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Nah, I have four childer...”
lines.note: 'Childer' is the recognizable Hiberno plural of child.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “...that are still alive, anyhow. Besides, I’m busy enough at the shop, so I am. But... what would it cost?”
lines.note: Grim aside continuation that forfeits a tell where the grief would inflect a Dubliner. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one light tell, e.g. 'sure I'm busy enough at the shop', keeping the grim restraint.

lines.speaker: dockworker
lines.span: “Fancy Boots… City of Paris. Remember the name. There’s the ship City of Paris. Ye’d have to go down to Cork to catch her. She’s no beauty, but if ye save yer pennies, it can be done. For you, Mr. Bootmaker, I’ve heard first class can be as low as fifteen or twenty pound!”
lines.note: 'Ye'd', 'yer pennies' and the uninflected 'pound' are solidly Dublin.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “We wouldn’t be goin’ first class. Sure don’t they just pile ye in the hold like herrin’ in a barrel? People are leavin’ in droves. It can’t be that dear at all.”
lines.note: 'Sure don't they...', 'dear' and 'at all' stack into thick Hiberno.

lines.speaker: dockworker
lines.span: “William, that’s... well, let’s just say ye’d best start savin’. With yer wife and all them childer... Last I heard it was six or seven pound a head.”
lines.note: 'them childer', 'yer wife' and uninflected 'pound' are richly Dublin.

lines.speaker: dockworker
lines.span: “You’re considerin’ this, aren’t ye?”
lines.note: The 'aren't ye?' Hiberno tag carries the question.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Aye. Do the Irish earn more money over there?”
lines.note: 'Aye' affirmative opener carries the otherwise plain question.

lines.speaker: dockworker
lines.span: “At least three times as much. Me cousin says he once made eighty United States dollars in a single month.”
lines.note: 'Me cousin' is the one clear Hiberno possessive tell.

lines.speaker: second laborer
lines.span: “It’s no paradise, mind,”
lines.note: The sentence-final 'mind' is a recognizable Hiberno tag.

lines.speaker: second laborer
lines.span: “People still get sick and die on ye. There’s still slums. And ye have to deal with a different class of people. A lot aren’t too fond of the Irish. Ye’d have to charm the divil himself to get on. It’s rough here, but we’ve got our own. That’s a fierce gamble.”
lines.note: 'die on ye', 'the divil himself' and 'a fierce gamble' make this thickly Hiberno.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Eighty dollars in a month? Then I’d best start savin’ tonight. Looks like you’re buyin’ the next round, lads! Sláinte!”
lines.note: 'lads' and the Irish toast 'Sláinte' anchor this firmly in Dublin.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Say it, then.”
lines.note: Three-word imperative, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “America.”
lines.note: Single word, no dialect signal.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “When?”
lines.note: One-word question, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “When I can pay for all of us. A year. Maybe two.”
lines.note: Clipped factual reply with no dialect opportunity forfeited.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “And go where?”
lines.note: Three-word question, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “New York first. Then wherever there’s work. I’ve heard Boston has Irish by the thousands. I could keep me craft. If not, I know there are farm hands, painters and laborers. I could learn a new trade. Start fresh.”
lines.note: A long earnest plan in flat standard English where a Dubliner would inflect at least once. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one tell, e.g. 'sure I could keep me craft' rather than 'my craft', without crowding the plan.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Six of us on a ship.”
lines.note: Short fearful fragment, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Six of us in America.”
lines.note: Mirroring fragment with no dialect signal.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “How are ye so certain at all that ye can pull this off?”
lines.note: Doubled 'ye' plus 'at all' make this thickly Dublin under strain.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Sure a man can do whatever he sets his mind to.”
lines.note: His credo lands as a flat textbook aphorism where a Dubliner would inflect. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one light tell, e.g. 'sure a man can do whatever he sets his mind to', keeping it a creed.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “George is buried here, William. Junior is buried here.”
lines.note: Bare grieving statement of fact, identical in any dialect; the restraint is right.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “I know. I cannot bury a third. They come with us. Every mile.”
lines.note: Spare grief-clipped lines; the plainness is earned, not a forfeited opportunity.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “No.”
lines.note: Single-word refusal, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I have followed you from Michael’s Lane to Stephen Street to this quay and to that quay and to the next one. I have scrubbed floors pregnant and wrung sheets until me hands bled and buried two children and bore four more. And I have said yes every time, William. Every time you stood in a doorway with that look on your face I have said yes.”
lines.note: A long passionate aria with full standard syntax and no tell where this Dublin wife would inflect. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one tell amid the catalogue, e.g. 'me hands' for 'my hands', a single touch so grief stays clean.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I can’t live with change like this. We have a community. I have a family. You keep asking me to change. It scares me, William. Ye are impossible! I’m sorry, but the answer is no. We have four children asleep on that floor and I will not put them on a ship because you saw the ships on the river and started counting.”
lines.note: 'Ye are impossible' carries one clear tell within an otherwise standard plea.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Then we stay,”
lines.note: Three-word concession, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “We stay in Ireland.”
lines.note: Bare reiteration with no dialect signal.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I walked to the end of the quay,”
lines.note: Plain narrative statement, identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I’ve been lookin’ at that river for fifteen years and I’ve never once watched where it goes.”
lines.note: A reflective full sentence with no tell where this Dublin woman would inflect at the turning point. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one light tell, e.g. 'never once, so I haven't' or 'watched where it goes at all', a single touch.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I’ll go, William. I’ll take the children and I’ll get on whatever ship ye find and I’ll go.”
lines.note: 'whatever ship ye find' supplies the one tell in this resolve.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “But I’m not going because ye’re chasing the sun. I’m going because I’ve been standing on this quay long enough, the ghosts of the boys have found me too.”
lines.note: The thematic title line lands in flat standard English with no Dublin inflection at a charged moment. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one restrained tell, e.g. 'the ghosts of the boys have found me too, so they have', kept singular so grief stays clean.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I won’t miss the smell,”
lines.note: Short functional line with no dialect signal either direction; identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “The River Liffey at low tide. That’s God’s honest punishment, so it is.”
lines.note: An expressive joke that forfeits a natural Dublin inflection a local would have taken. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one tell so the quip lands as Dublin, e.g. a fronting or 'Sure' opener, one tell only.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I’ll miss the light, though.”
lines.note: Brief line with sentence-final 'though' but no positive dialect signal; functional and dialect-neutral.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “The way it comes through of an afternoon, just before the rain.”
lines.note: An expressive descriptive line that forfeits an Irish opportunity (habitual 'does be coming through') a Dubliner would take. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Lean on habitual aspect so the recurring light reads Dublin, one tell only, no triad.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “There’s light where we’re goin’, Mary. Different light. Wider.”
lines.note: Tender reassurance long enough to forfeit a natural Dublin inflection; flat textbook-English. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add a single tell to the reassurance (e.g. 'Sure there's light...') without stacking beats.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “Ye don’t know that.”
lines.note: Carries the Hiberno 'Ye'; already doing its job.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “No. But I believe it.”
lines.note: Short functional reply with no dialect signal either direction.

lines.speaker: clerk
lines.span: “Names?”
lines.note: One-word functional prompt; excluded from averages.

lines.speaker: Letitia
lines.span: “Letitia Boog. And this is Teresa.”
lines.note: Bare names plus a simple introduction; dialect-neutral and not a fair scoring target for a four-year-old.

lines.speaker: William
lines.span: “Ready?”
lines.note: One-word functional question; identical in any dialect.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.span: “I’m thinkin’ I was ready since Michael’s Lane.”
lines.note: A reflective, emotionally weighted line that forfeits a natural Dublin inflection a local would take. [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: Add one tell to warm it into Dublin (e.g. an after-perfect or 'sure'), single tell, no triad.

lines.speaker: Mary
lines.note: fixed prayer-oath surfacing in awe, a correctly pitched short slip
lines.span: Mother of God

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: to family, Hiberno negation plus dropped g lands thick
lines.span: We’ll not be stayin’ here,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: [re-scan 2026-06-18] now carries a tell (only (Hiberno qualifier = merely)); fix applied to Drive, register lifted to irish.
lines.span: This is only the door. Not the house.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: fresh-off-boat 2a baseline reads fully American to the clerk
lines.fixDirection: re-Irish toward an Act-2a surviving tell
lines.span: New York or Boston,

fixes.replace: New York or Boston, please God.
fixes.note: William, ch10 L648 (re-do). UNDER-IRISH: ‘please God’ — the everyday Irish-Catholic hope-tag (= God willing), natural in a man at the threshold of a new life. One warm tell to the clerk.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: [re-scan 2026-06-18] now carries a tell (-in' (dropped-g)); fix applied to Drive, register lifted to irish.
lines.span: I was a bootmaker. Now lookin’ for laborer work, or maybe a painter.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: guarded challenge is thick Dublin, right on the 2a baseline
lines.span: What would ye be wantin’ with me money?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: deliberate mimicry of American greeting, flat is the point
lines.span: How do you do?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: deliberate mimicry to blend in, flat is intentional
lines.span: Afternoon, sir.

lines.speaker: Cork man
lines.note: countryman to countryman, ye plus Hiberno tag question
lines.span: Easy, brother. I can hear the Dublin on ye from ten feet away. Just off the boat, are ye?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: terse defensive line carries one tell, fits the clipped beat
lines.span: What’s it to ye?

lines.speaker: Cork man
markers: God and Mary be with you (blessing)
lines.note: holy blessing is the slip surfacing on disarming
lines.span: Nothing to me, I come in peace. God and Mary be with you.

lines.speaker: Cork man
lines.note: shared-arrival memory pulls the dialect up, well pitched
lines.span: You stood out to me. Lovely family ye’ve got. I was you, four years ago. Standing right where ye’re standing with the same look on me face.

lines.speaker: Cork man
lines.note: [re-scan 2026-06-18] now carries a tell (ye); fix applied to Drive, register lifted to irish.
lines.span: Listen. Are ye a tradesman?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: memory of the Liffey shop plus a working-class idiom, thick
lines.span: Bootmaker. I had my own shop on the Liffey. I heard I could make a better living with painting. I’ll chance me arm on that.

lines.speaker: Cork man
lines.note: alarmed warning brings ye plus a Hiberno idiom
lines.span: Are ye gone soft in the head? This place will chew you up and spit you out.

lines.speaker: Cork man
lines.note: [re-scan 2026-06-18] now carries a tell (-in' (dropped-g), ye); fix applied to Drive, register lifted to irish.
lines.span: Americans are not too fond of us Irish. The dream is dead for us, I’m afraid. I’m goin’ back to our homeland. Ye should consider the same.
