lines.span: I don’t think so. I don’t know.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: invoking the children's dead mother with 'yer mother'; a tell surfaces, correctly pitched dip
lines.span: What about yer mother?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat American
lines.span: I’ll bring her over for dinner again.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat American
lines.span: Kids, I think Miss Lottie and I should talk about that first.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: playful threat with three dropped-g/fer tells clustered; jocular family register reads as affectionate, acceptable dip
lines.span: Girls, you’re itchin’ fer a clippin’.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat American relenting
lines.span: Okay, okay, okay, I agree, I think we should all get together, but this house can’t hold everyone. We’d have to plan the food—

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat American call to eat
lines.span: Kids, let’s eat.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat American
lines.span: Who?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: helpless with laughter; short flat utterance, light register, slip not required for two words
lines.span: Sorry. I can’t.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: off-guard with laughter, 'yerselves' surfaces; correctly pitched light slip
lines.span: Watch yerselves, kids, I heard them bees can do the backstroke!

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: 'Ye’re very good' single tell, on band
lines.span: Ye’re very good. Mr. and Mrs.…

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat American thanks
lines.span: Nice to meet you. We ran from a swarm of bees. These towels are great. Thanks again for them.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat American
lines.span: Do you think we should all get together again?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: 'Grand' signature vocab tic, within band
lines.span: Grand. I think we can arrange that. For now, I’d rather not ride home in the dark.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: opening a serious talk; flat measured tone fits a man bracing himself, slip not yet warranted
lines.span: Charlotte, I want to talk with you about something.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: reflecting on rise from nothing; 'more than grand' tic surfaces under emotion, correctly pitched
lines.span: Yes, it is. And in truth that is what keeps me up at night. Everything is more than grand. I was brought into this world with nothing. Now I am sitting in a carriage in the United States with a lovely woman who my children adore. I have money, a successful business and respect.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: naming the buried seven kids and a wife is the deepest grief beat in the chapter; flat American misses the slip
lines.fixDirection: let one old tell surface on the count of the dead, dip toward band floor
lines.span: But this… all came at a cost. Charlotte, I have buried seven kids and a wife.

fixes.replace: seven kids and me wife
fixes.note: L1790 — me-for-my on the count of the dead, his deepest clause; everything else stays American.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: reckoning grace under grief; 'Sure' opener used right plus the idiomatic 'ought not to be well' is a proper E slip
lines.span: Sure, things ought not to be well, but they are. Better than I deserve.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat practical instruction to the driver
lines.span: Please stop here. Lottie, step down with me.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat American question
lines.span: How do you take care of those six kids by yourself?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the proposal lead-in carries the syntax idiom 'Would ye ever' (weight 3) under emotion; deep E slip, correctly pitched for the most intimate beat
lines.span: Would ye ever be willing to put up with a man? Let’s say a man with skeletons in his closet?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: calling her name; single word, no slip possible
lines.span: Charlotte.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the formal proposal; flat formal phrasing is intentional for a marriage proposal, the prior line carried the slip
lines.span: Charlotte, will you take my hand in marriage?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat name call
lines.span: Lottie?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: brief instruction; the slip lands on the following line invoking Mary
lines.span: Don’t put it back up,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: memory of the late Mary; 'Ah sure' opener is a textbook E slip, correctly pitched
lines.span: Ah sure, let the house sort itself out. Mary never had a chart. She just showed up every morning and fought through it.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat declaration
lines.span: This is it,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat wry American
lines.span: Of course you did.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat retort
lines.span: Most painters work for someone else,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: resisting loss of her name; flat insistence fits the controlled grief, the formal full name carries weight
lines.span: That’s not your name. Your name is Letitia Agnes Boog.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: invoking the dead Mary's understanding at a parting; flat American leaves the memory-slip unused [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: let an old tell surface invoking Mary, dip toward band floor
lines.span: Yer mother would have understood this,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: naming the convent; single fragment, no slip room
lines.span: St. Catherine’s,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: 'a good place at all' carries the sentence-final 'at all' filler under emotion; correctly pitched light slip
lines.span: Is it a good place at all?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the crossing from Ireland on the City of Paris is the deepest Ireland-memory beat; flat delivery misses an E slip [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: let one phonetic tell surface on the manifest memory, dip toward band floor
lines.span: You were the first name on the manifest, you know. You and Teresa. Passengers one and two on the City of Paris. Four years old and ye didn’t cry.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: gentle correction; three words, slip not required
lines.span: No you don’t.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: tearful 'Ye’d better' at parting; the tell surfaces under emotion, correctly pitched
lines.span: Ye’d better.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat curious question
lines.span: Where did you learn that?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat self-definition
lines.span: I’m a painter, Rose. Not an artist.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: tender memory question; flat American but warm, the pocket question on the next line carries the slip
lines.span: Rose, when you were a little girl, do you remember what I asked you often?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: affectionate 'would ye ever let me keep ye' carries the idiom plus pronoun under tenderness; pitched lighter than the proposal but correctly a slip
lines.span: I asked if I could put you in my pocket, so you’d always be with me. So would ye ever let me keep ye in my pocket?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: describing the dead Mary to a dying daughter, the highest-grief context in the act; flat American is a missed slip [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: let an old tell surface describing Mary, dip toward band floor
lines.span: She was the strongest person I ever knew, so she was. She could make a home out of any room. Four walls and your mother, and you had everything you needed. She always did what others wanted, even if it wasn’t her first choice. She hated change, but I think that changed over time.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: continuing the Mary eulogy; the 'four walls and your mother' idiom-flavored line carries warmth, defensible flat
lines.span: She could make a home out of any room. Four walls and your mother, and you had everything you needed. She always did what others wanted, even if it wasn’t her first choice. She hated change, but I think that changed over time.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: barely holding together; short flat answer is dramatically right at the breaking point
lines.span: She loved the sky.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: non-verbal grunt; W license available but a grunt carries no dialect, scoring as flat non-issue
lines.span: Hrmm.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: moved by his daughter's painting of the Liffey (Dublin river); flat American on a pure Ireland-memory beat misses the slip [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: let a tell surface on naming the Liffey, dip toward band floor
lines.span: You painted the aul’ Liffey,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: marveling about Dublin; 'Ye’ve never seen Dublin' surfaces the tell under emotion, correctly pitched
lines.span: Ye’ve never seen Dublin.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: fragment about the sign; slip lands on the recited Dublin address that follows
lines.span: The sign on the building,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: two-word fragment, no slip room
lines.span: Above the door.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: naming his old Dublin shop sign; the proper-noun recitation is its own kind of slip, content carries it
lines.span: W. Boog. Bootmaker.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: reciting the Dublin address from memory; place-name recall carries the Ireland charge
lines.span: Number thirteen Aston’s Quay.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: choked two-word plea; no slip room
lines.span: Don’t stop,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: single-word name call
lines.span: Rose.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the Dublin-light praise; flat phrasing but emotionally landed, the repetition does the work
lines.span: The light was right. On the water. You got the light exactly right.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: broaching the longing for a son; guarded flat opener is right for a subject he can barely name
lines.span: Lottie, you have nine children. Three by me.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: deflecting, unable to name his wish; flat trailing-off fits the evasion
lines.span: I don’t have any big plans. I just…

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: admitting the painful subject; 'Sure, it’s a painful subject' uses the opener right under emotion, proper E slip
lines.span: Sure, it’s a painful subject. I’d be surprised if I can even produce a healthy boy at this point.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: realizing a second daughter leaves; two-word stunned fragment, no slip room
lines.span: You too,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: pressing Rose want-vs-calling; repeated 'ye want'/'ye feel'/'ye want' clusters the tell under emotion, correctly pitched
lines.span: Is this what ye want, Rose? Not what ye feel called to. What ye want.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: promise to cherish the painting; flat but tender, defensible
lines.span: I’ll cherish your painting forever.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: 'yer family' single tell, on band
lines.span: They’re yer family, Lottie.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: a folk-vulgar idiom but a general Americanism not Hiberno; reads as blunt William, on band
lines.span: You can hope in one hand, crap in the other, see which one fills first.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: 'Have ye written' single tell, on band
lines.span: Have ye written to them?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat business decision
lines.span: We foreclose,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat dismissive
lines.span: Good for the McCarthys.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat dismissal of the telephone
lines.span: You can talk to someone in Saint Paul by writing them a letter and waiting a week. What’s the hurry?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat American 'his own two eyes' is general idiom not Hiberno
lines.span: A man should know if his shipment arrived by going to the station and looking at it with his own two eyes.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat refusal
lines.span: Absolutely not.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat startled question
lines.span: You tell a woman?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat echo
lines.span: Plugs the wires.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat question
lines.span: Who do I get connected with?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat number
lines.span: Seven fourteen,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat shouted introduction, comedic
lines.span: HELLO, JACKSON. THIS IS WILLIAM BOOG. I AM AT THE MCCARTHY RESIDENCE. CAN YOU HEAR ME?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat insistence, comedic
lines.span: I AM NOT SHOUTING. I AM SPEAKING CLEARLY SO THAT MY VOICE CARRIES THE DISTANCE.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: 'Good day to ye' single surviving tell, on band
lines.span: I don’t trust this contraption, Jackson. Tell Frank his machine works. Good day to ye.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat one-word
lines.span: What?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat astonishment
lines.span: That’s all you do?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat
lines.span: You just talk? Regular voice?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat technical disbelief
lines.span: But how does it carry? There’s no power behind it. It’s a wire, Lottie. A wire doesn’t carry a voice from here to Saint Paul without force.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat
lines.span: I don’t like it,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat insistence
lines.span: The thing is broken. It shouldn’t work that way.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat one-word
lines.span: Well,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat self-deprecating joke, no dialect tell
lines.span: if the whole city heard that, at least they know the name Boog.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat concession
lines.span: I know it is.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: sentence-final 'now' is a faint Hiberno tag but very light; on band
lines.span: Let me think on it now.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: lamenting the speeding world; reflective but flat, the inventory is delivered in modern register, defensible
lines.span: The world is speeding up, Lottie. Electric lights on the roads. Streetcars running without horses. Black discs that play music when a needle goes across them. Now a box on the wall that carries your voice across the city.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: reflecting on hand-and-handshake life; flat but the cadence (short imperatives) carries it
lines.span: I’ve spent my entire life using my hands and my voice to build things. Walk up to a man. Shake his hand. Look him in the eye. That’s how I got every contract I ever won. Every property I ever bought. Will those days change?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the act's thematic gut-punch tying the effortless wire to the children he could not save; deep grief, flat American leaves the slip on the table
lines.fixDirection: let one old tell surface where he names the lost children, dip toward band floor
lines.span: That world is getting smaller. The one where a man does business with his hands and his word. A voice carried clear to Saint Paul on a copper wire, and there was nothing behind it. No lungs, no man leaning in. I’ve spent my whole life believing nothing carries unless you put your back into it. All my pushing never carried the children through. I pushed as hard as a man can, and three of them slipped away regardless. And a copper wire moves a voice across a city without so much as trying.

fixes.find: All my pushing never carried the children through.
fixes.replace: All me pushing never carried the children through.
fixes.note: L2168 — me-for-my landing on the children he could not save (the wire-vs-grief gut-punch).

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: mid-panic abrupt excuse; two words, no slip room
lines.span: Excuse me,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: deflecting his daughter after the attack; flat 'I’m fine' is a guard going back up, dramatically right
lines.span: I’m fine,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: fragment opening the confession; slip lands on the long beat that follows
lines.span: When I look at them,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: naming every buried child of Dublin and Boston, the act's deepest grief monologue; near-flat with only the Dublin/Boston place-content; one phonetic tell would pull it into proper slip [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: let a single old tell surface amid the count of the dead, dip toward band floor
lines.span: When I look at them at that table I see every child I’ve buried. Every one. George and William in Dublin, Joseph, Austin, Teresa, in Boston, John, all of them, God rest them. I look at Charlotte and I see Teresa. I look at Alex and I see John. The chair Louise is sitting in is the chair Austin sat in. They’re not the same children, I know that. But my mind doesn’t know it. My mind sees a full table and it counts the empty chairs from every table I’ve ever sat at and it puts them all in the same room.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the 'full table is the most dangerous thing' confession, raw grief; flat American misses the slip
lines.fixDirection: let one old tell surface in the confession, dip toward band floor
lines.span: A full table around me is the most dangerous thing in the world. Every seat is a seat that can go empty. I’ve watched it happen so many times that when I see a room full of people I love, I can’t see them as they are. I see them as they could be. Gone. And I’m still sitting there.

fixes.replace: And me still sitting there.
fixes.note: L2195 — the Hiberno "and me + -ing" absolute on the gutting final image of the confession.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat dismissal, guard restored
lines.span: She shouldn’t be.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat instruction, composing himself
lines.span: Go back inside,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat instruction
lines.span: Tell them I needed air.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: fragment opening; slip carried by the following 'rooms' beat
lines.span: My mind goes to rooms,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: describing the nightly rooms; flat but the controlled horror is dramatically right, place-names follow
lines.span: Rooms you don’t want to know exist. I go there every night. Sometimes during the day. I went there Sunday at this table, with everyone sitting right here.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: naming Glasnevin and Mary's sickbed, deepest Ireland/grief memory of the act; only place-content carries it, one tell would make the slip land
lines.fixDirection: let one old tell surface naming Glasnevin or Mary, dip toward band floor
lines.span: The rooms are real places. Glasnevin. Stephen Street. The flat in Boston where Joseph died. The bedroom where Mary told me she was sick. I don’t choose to go there. I’m just there. And every time I come back it takes longer. I used to be able to shake it off and get to work. Lately I can’t shake it at all. And I know what is about to happen.

fixes.find: Lately I can’t shake it at all.
fixes.replace: Lately I can’t shake it at all, at all.
fixes.note: L2213 — the doubled "at all, at all" as he names Glasnevin and Mary’s sickbed and admits he is losing his grip. (Heaviest of the set; reject if too much.)

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: stark certainty Alex and Lottie will die; flat is dramatically chilling, defensible
lines.span: If I stay here, Alex will die. You will too.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: recalling his infants and seven-year-old dying; 'anythin’ that I love that is near me, dies' surfaces the dropped-g under peak grief, proper deep E slip
lines.span: You’re saying this to a man who watched two of his boys die in his arms at eleven months old. You’ve never heard a baby stop moaning but the floor continues to creak from a rocking chair. But I have. Once, I held the hand of my seven year old son, he gasped for air until he was too weak. I know that bad things happen once in a while. But anythin’ that I love that is near me, dies Lottie.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: God's-punishment cry; two dropped-g tells ('punishin'','gettin'') cluster under rage/grief, proper E slip
lines.span: I am alone in it. It’s God’s way of punishin’ me, I have mocked and rejected him, maybe I am only gettin’ what I deserve

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: counting his children's odds and calling himself a curse to his only son, harrowing; flat American beside the slipped lines around it reads inconsistent
lines.fixDirection: let one dropped-g or old tell surface to match the surrounding slip, dip toward band floor
lines.span: I look at Charlotte and I count the years she has left based on how long Teresa lasted. I do that. I look at Alex and I calculate the odds. He is my only Boog son. I am a curse to him. Our baby is asleep upstairs right now and I have already buried him in my mind a hundred times. That is not something you can manage with a doctor or a priest.

fixes.find: He is my only Boog son.
fixes.replace: He is me only Boog son.
fixes.note: L2223 — me-for-my on Alex, matching the dropped-g slips in the lines around it (anythin’, punishin’).

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: pitching Grants Pass as a businessman; deliberately flat planning register, guard up, on band
lines.span: I’ve been reading about southern Oregon. The Rogue Valley. A town called Grants Pass. Timber country, mining, orchards going in along every creek bottom. Twenty-three hundred people. The whole place is building and there’s nobody to paint it. Real estate is a fraction of what it costs here. I need to get away and this spot seems like it was built for me.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: fragment; the declarative that follows carries it, flat is controlled
lines.span: You have it wrong, Lottie,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: framing departure as protection; the brisk military 'executing the only plan' is intentionally guarded, defensible flat
lines.span: I am not running from the dead. I am executing the only plan I have left to protect the living.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat protest, three words
lines.span: That’s not fair.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the 100%-certainty line (Ch31 callback per LANGUAGE-PASS); intentionally flat and absolute, the cadence is the point, keep
lines.span: It’s not often in my life that I have a true certainty. But when I do, Lottie, I am correct 100% of the time. And the math on my own blood is absolute.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: promising properties and money; transactional reassurance, flat fits the businessman pivot
lines.span: Far from it, Lottie. Not only do you and Alex get both of these properties, and all other properties we have in the area, I will buy you land out west. I will send money back to you. You are right, Lottie, you have sacrificed a lot. Together we can build something great for our family. But you have to trust me, I need to be away or else it will all fall to pieces.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the compass-for-Alex reveal, tender; flat but the content is intimate, the next lines and frame carry the weight
lines.span: Lottie, I bought the compass for Alex. I bought well before he was born, before we had money, in fact when I first arrived in Saint Paul. Give it to him when he turns 7.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the widow/census instruction (will-census beat); 'Here’s what ye do' single tell, cold planning register, on band
lines.span: Here’s what ye do. To the outside world, I passed away. You are a widow. When the census person comes, that is what you say. A widow gets no questions and a lot of grace. Legally we are still married, it will make the deeds work.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: another certainty about the kids outliving him; flat absolute cadence consistent with his certainty register
lines.span: Our poor kids? They should be thankful. They’ll all outlive me with me gone. Another one of my certainties. I’m not going to Oregon as a cure. I’m going to protect and provide.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: three-word unmovable declaration; flat is the wall going up, dramatically right
lines.span: I’m going anyway.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: single-word name; cut off, no slip room
lines.span: Lottie.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: three-word promise; flat is fine
lines.span: I won’t lie.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the climactic love declaration as he gives her up; the act's emotional peak, flat American misses an earned slip [regraded: slip applied to Drive]
lines.fixDirection: let one old tell surface in the love declaration, dip toward band floor
lines.span: Lottie, you are so beautiful and I love you more than you know. I’m giving up the greatest thing in me life for you and our bundles of joy.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: two-word depot promise; no slip room
lines.span: I’ll write,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: final promise of property in the children's names; flat is the controlled goodbye, defensible
lines.span: I’ll buy property in their names. Every one of them.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat wry American, correct for late-act baseline
lines.span: Last time, if it’s as good as they say.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat transactional American
lines.span: I want to run an advertisement.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: written advertising copy, fully American register
lines.span: William Boog of the Grants Pass Painting Co. Inventor and Manufacturer of Fire-Proof and Anti-Rust Iron, Tin and Metal Paint. Can be used interior and exterior, as well as on shingles. No need to mortgage your house to have it painted by strangers. Have it done by the old reliable who never breaks his word even when it costs time and money. Send postal card and I will act promptly.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: confident American sales talk, no tells
lines.span: I’ve been mixing paint since before you were born. I know exactly what goes in it because I put it there myself.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat American complaint about local work
lines.span: People out here are paying too much for bad work. I’ve seen the buildings. Half the paint in this town is peeling and fading inside two years. A man should not have to go broke to get his house painted properly.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat defiant American
lines.span: Until I tell you to stop.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat American
lines.span: That’s me.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat American sales reasoning
lines.span: That’s because most painters in this county are painting with someone else’s formula. I make my own. A gallon of good oil paint runs a dollar and a half. You know what they’re charging you for the cheap stuff that streaks after one winter? About the same. The difference is mine holds.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: echoes the American ad slogan
lines.span: Send a postal card and I will act promptly.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: formal American politeness, no tells
lines.span: I beg your pardon, sir, but you’ll have to leave a postcard. I’ll find room for you in the calendar.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: brief flat reflexive question, too short to surge, license held in reserve
lines.span: What does that mean?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat bitter refusal, brief enough to stay flat in late act
lines.span: He’ll just make it worse,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: one-word mask; flatness is the point, no slip warranted
lines.span: Fine.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: famine-rage eruption surges to ye/young-fella, a deep-memory slip done right
lines.span: You don’t say that, young fella! There are people starving all over the world. Be grateful ye have a mother who takes care of ye!

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: one broken word; flat is correct, nothing to surge
lines.span: Why?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: saying her name; flat by nature
lines.span: Lottie.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: confession of the ruined hip slips to workin'/me head, pitched right for grief
lines.span: I fell off a ladder. Four years ago. My hip… My hip is finished. I can’t climb, I shouldn’t be workin’ above me head. There’s this surgeon in Portland. I’m not going.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: deepest dread of doctors and the dead, but stays flat American with no tell
lines.fixDirection: let one surviving pronoun tell (ye/me) surface in this grief beat
lines.span: Because every doctor I’ve ever sat across from has told me someone I love is going to die. I’d rather walk with a stick.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: short broken admission; flatness reads as numbness, acceptable
lines.span: I can’t stop seeing them,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: naming the Dublin/Boston ghosts is the oldest memory yet stays flat
lines.fixDirection: re-Irish lightly; one tell as the Dublin image lands
lines.span: The children. The rooms. The water in Boston. The blankets in Dublin. Mary in the chair.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: grief-exhaustion confession; flat broken cadence works
lines.span: I could outrun it when I was working. I could paint until I couldn’t think. The ladder was the only place it didn’t follow me. Now the ladder is gone and I’m just...

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: uses her formal name Charlotte; deliberate distance, flat fits
lines.span: I’m sitting here, Charlotte. Every day. Sitting here with all of it.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: barely-audible tenderness; brief flat line acceptable
lines.span: I’ve always liked that,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat refusal; short, no surge needed
lines.span: You can’t fix this, Lottie.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat; nearby line 2532 carries the chapter's slip load
lines.span: Being here brings it all back.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: long grief monologue but stays controlled American; slip is held for 2532
lines.span: You bring Minnesota with you. The house. The kids. The whole life. Fake perfection. And when I look at you I see all of it and I can’t hold it all at once. I’ve been carrying it one piece at a time my whole life. Now it’s all in the room.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: hoarse two-word concession; flat correct
lines.span: You’re right.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat confession of being broken; restraint fits
lines.span: I don’t know how to fix what’s broken in me, Lottie.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat fatalist insistence; surge reserved for 2532
lines.span: Lottie, you don’t understand, they will certainly die if I come back. I can’t be convinced otherwise.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat resigned reply
lines.span: I don’t know anymore,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the achievements-summary is deliberately American-provider register
lines.span: That’s the honest answer. I built everything a man is supposed to build. I have the deeds and the bank accounts and the name on the sign. My life is to protect and provide now. The Boogs will be safe and will live good lives.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat denial, immediately followed by the deep slip in 2532b; pairing works
lines.span: I’m not quitting.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the chapter's deepest grief surges to ye/me eyes/me carry, exemplary E slip
lines.span: There’s a difference. You can’t win against death. You can fight it and fight it and the thing just keeps taking. If ye knew what I carry, Lottie. If ye knew the rooms I go to when I close me eyes. Winning isn’t an option. It never was. Life has taken too much from me and I can’t take one ounce of it back.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: surviving 'Sure' assent as she leaves; a correct tic-level slip
lines.span: Sure, I know,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat two-word close; restraint after the surge fits
lines.span: I know.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: old man's friendly American chatter, no tells
lines.span: Nice building you’ve got here. You know, I painted this fifteen years ago. I’m William Boog.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat American observation
lines.span: I was watching a hawk today, soaring for miles without ever having to flap its wings. You ever see that?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: American property-advice patter; 'lovely' here is generic, not a marker
lines.span: Here’s a tip on how to live like that hawk. Get some more property, rent this lovely building out. You will make money without ever having to flap your wings.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat American invitation
lines.span: If you change your mind, I live just off of G street. Come find me. I can walk you through it.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat American farewell
lines.span: You’re welcome, I hope today is a great day for you.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat stubborn American refusal of surgery
lines.span: I’ve walked this far with a stick,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat American close
lines.span: I’ll walk the rest.

lines.speaker: Doyle
lines.note: remembered Dublin line; thick Hiberno is correct as a memory among his own
lines.span: In with ye, boy.

lines.speaker: Mary
markers: Hiberno cadence (seems like a good door to stand in)
lines.note: remembered Boston-era Mary line; gentle idiom, pitched right for memory
lines.span: Seems like a good door to stand in.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: first prayer in sixty years; bitter Dublin cadence to God is a correct slip
lines.span: What do you want with a bastard like me?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: naming his seven dead is the oldest grief in the book yet stays flat
lines.fixDirection: let one old tell surface as he counts the dead (e.g. God rest them)
lines.span: Seven. Seven children, a stillborn, some miscarriages too.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the 'where I came from' Ireland-memory beat is exactly where Dublin should surge
lines.fixDirection: re-Irish lightly as Ireland is named (a ye/me tell)
lines.span: And a wife. Across three cities and fifty years. You asked me earlier where I came from. That’s where I came from. That’s what I carried here.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat reflective American to the lawyer
lines.span: I’ve had a few failed gambles, Hollis. Boston was a bad one. This gamble seems to have paid off.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: proud roll-call of living children; joy stays in American register, acceptable
lines.span: Every last one of them, Hollis. My daughters from Mary are both nuns and both of them paint. Charlotte, Louise and Virginia are all married. And before you ask, yes, even Alex, married his high school sweetheart Angela.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: voice shaking but brief; flat acceptable
lines.span: I’m happy to report, yes,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: proud declaration; flat American pride fits the frame
lines.span: I am a proud grandfather of many.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat legal dictation of deeds
lines.span: The deeds on Carroll and Roblyn, and my second house here in Grants Pass. They belong to Alex.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: will dictation; deliberately precise American; the final clause carries weight without dialect
lines.span: Split my bank assets as follows, 60% to Alex Boog, as he will take care of Lottie. He will live next door to her. 10% go to my oldest Boog grandson, Stewart Boog. Another 10% will go to my other grandson, named after me, William Boog. The remaining 20% will be split among all other grandchildren. No Boog will start from nothing ever again.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat legal dictation
lines.span: My other children will have properties, jewelry and bonds.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the night his first son was born in Dublin is a deep-origin memory, left flat
lines.fixDirection: re-Irish lightly as Dublin is named (one old tell)
lines.span: I started this paper the night my first son was born in Dublin,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: plain confession of intent; flat fits the quiet beat
lines.span: I didn’t know what it would become. I just wanted to write the names down.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat grave-bound declaration; the plainness lands
lines.span: This goes in the ground with me.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat statement of purpose
lines.span: The names are in the will now. That’s what I came here for.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: oblique reference to the church door; deliberately understated, flat works
lines.span: Let’s just say, a door opened for me at the right time.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat anxious admission about Alex; brief, acceptable
lines.span: I wasn’t sure he’d come,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat bequest to his son; emotion carried by content not dialect
lines.span: The Greenwood house is yours,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat practical bequest
lines.span: Clear, in your name. And most of what I leave besides. It’ll help you take care of your mother.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat pragmatic explanation of the widow ploy
lines.span: I instructed her to do that. More social graces and better benefits. I knew you’d take care of her. That’s why I am leaving so much to you.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: compass memory from when he had nothing; tender but stays American; passes for the frame
lines.span: I bought that when I had nothing to my name, a decade before you were born. I wanted to know that my son could find his way in life.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat three-word instruction
lines.span: Give it to William.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: reflection on the burden of the name; gentle American, acceptable
lines.span: A name like that can be a hard thing to carry. Maybe the compass helps him land somewhere good.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: surviving signature 'Grand' approval; exactly the kind of tic Act 5 keeps
lines.span: Grand,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: Hiberno blessing as he parts; surviving farewell tic, keep
lines.span: God bless ye, Mr. Hollis.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: final words to the sun; the surviving phonetic tell closes the arc, keep
lines.span: Found ya.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: flat assent, near-American baseline as the frame allows
lines.span: I appreciate that.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: long emotional origin-memory rendered flat; a faint me-mother slip is earned.
lines.fixDirection: let one old tell surface on the name/father memory
lines.span: It doesn’t come from anywhere. That’s the honest answer. My mother chose the name William. Nobody handed her a family Bible with Boog written in the front. No grandfather passed it down over a Sunday dinner. It came from a Scottish father that I never knew. Except I heard they spelled it differently, B-o-a-g from what I was told. I was raised by distant cousins but they spelled it B-o-o-g.

fixes.find: My mother chose the name William.
fixes.replace: Me mother chose the name William.
fixes.note: me for my — one faint slip on his deepest clause; the Dublin ghost the narration says is not fully erased.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: sentence fragment, no natural opportunity on its own; the unit is the next clause.
lines.fixDirection: re-Irish the Dublin-birth line with a surviving phonetic tell
lines.span: I was born in a tenement on Stephen Street in Dublin,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: mother-memory left flat; carries the me-mother slip.
lines.fixDirection: let the guard drop with one me-for-my or 'tis tell
lines.span: November of 1841. My mother was on her own. She was young. The tenements had a way of making age difficult to judge.

fixes.find: My mother was on her own.
fixes.replace: Me mother was on her own.
fixes.note: same tell, kept consistent (he says me mother both times); everything else stays American.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: tender mother-memory; one light me-for-my slip earned.
lines.fixDirection: re-Irish lightly on the mother memory
lines.span: She chose my Catholic name herself. Weeks before I arrived. I don’t know why she picked William.

fixes.find: She chose my Catholic name herself.
fixes.replace: She chose me Catholic name herself.
fixes.note: me for my, one light slip on the mother-memory; avoids a Sure-on-declarative.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: already carries at all — a Hiberno tell. Not a miss.
lines.fixDirection: let a wee/at all tell slip in on this beat
lines.span: It was no place at all to raise a child,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: all the same is generic English, no opportunity. Neutral.
lines.fixDirection: let one phonetic tell carry the affection for his mother
lines.span: but she did it all the same.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: pacing deflection, correctly flat baseline
lines.span: I’ll get to that. After that, things moved quickly.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: one-word death toll, the flatness is the grief-control and reads as intentional restraint
lines.span: Six,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: refusing the offered moment over six dead children, flat American
lines.fixDirection: a single old tell would let the grief crack through
lines.span: I don’t need a moment.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: grief-and-work credo, deeply off-guard, yet wholly American syntax
lines.fixDirection: let the boots/work lines slip with a phonetic or me-for-my tell
lines.span: Grief doesn’t excuse you from lacing your boots the next morning. It doesn’t make your children who are still alive any less hungry. You get up. You go to work. You paint the wall in front of you.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: rejecting the bravery label, emotional self-account, flat (naming Hollis is not a marker)
lines.fixDirection: re-Irish lightly so the rebuff carries the old voice
lines.span: That is not bravery, Mr. Hollis. That is just taking care of what’s in front of you.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: expository turning-point, correctly flat baseline
lines.span: An assessment. If you’re not prepared for an unplanned expense, it can ruin you,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: continued exposition framing expense as opportunity, flat baseline as allowed
lines.span: But if you’re lucky enough, an unplanned expense can make you consider your options.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: plot transition, near-American baseline correct for the frame
lines.span: I didn’t. But an unplanned expense started a trajectory I never saw coming.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the lost children weigh under the count and 'Aye' lets one old tell surface, a correctly-pitched slip
lines.span: Aye, yes, two children on that train,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: 'me children's names' surfaces the surviving me-for-my tell on a grief-weighted line, a well-pitched slip
lines.span: Mr. Hollis, you are writing a will. So I need you to understand who gets what and why. And I need you to understand that the list of me children’s names is longer than what I’ve told you so far.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: one-word weight of the lost children; restraint reads as intentional, not a missed slip
lines.span: Longer.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: opening the thousand-mile-west memory, a prime slip beat left flat
lines.fixDirection: let the journey memory slip with a phonetic or 'tis tell
lines.span: We took that train a thousand miles it seems…

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: naming Hollis, flat baseline correct
lines.span: Hollis.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: Mary and Dublin in view, the most slip-prone subject, voiced flat
lines.fixDirection: let one old tell surface as Mary/Dublin come up
lines.span: She had not.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: insisting Mary chose to come, defensive grief, flat American
lines.fixDirection: re-Irish lightly so the defense of Mary carries the old voice
lines.span: She chose to come.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: gone still with the question, rising emotion, no slip surfaces
lines.fixDirection: let the stillness break into one phonetic tell
lines.span: What are you asking me, Hollis?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: hoarse opening of the deepest confession, flat
lines.fixDirection: let the hoarse beat slip with an old tell
lines.span: Every day,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: raw-voiced confession that he wore Mary out, the rawest beat in the frame, flat American
lines.fixDirection: let the guard fully drop here with a phonetic tell
lines.span: Every day since November of 1887 I have asked myself that question. But you have the sums wrong, Hollis. It wasn’t the life or the work that wore her out. It was me.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the fortress-and-curse confession naming his parents, deepest off-guard, yet flat
lines.fixDirection: let the spite-against-parents memory slip into the old voice
lines.span: I built a fortress from deeds and paint just to prove to a father and mother who discarded me that the name Boog was worth something. I thought if I built the wall high enough, nothing could reach us. But a wall doesn’t protect a house when the poison is already locked inside the gates. I know what I did, Hollis. I know the cost. I have been paying it for thirty-eight years. I am the curse.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: Mary on the porch giving permission, tender memory, flat
lines.fixDirection: re-Irish the porch memory with one surviving tell
lines.span: She told me not to stop,

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: continuing the porch-warning memory of Mary, flat American
lines.fixDirection: let the porch beat slip with a phonetic tell
lines.span: On the porch. She told me not to stop chasing whatever I was chasing. She said it would kill me if I tried.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: insisting Mary knew the cost and was no fool, fierce protective grief, flat
lines.fixDirection: let the defense of Mary carry one old tell
lines.span: That was her giving me permission to keep going. It was also her telling me she knew what it had cost her. She knew, Hollis. She was not a fool. She was the furthest thing from a fool.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the love-and-used-her-up confession lets 'dyin'' surface, a correctly-pitched single-tell slip on the rawest love beat
lines.span: I loved her more than I have ever loved anything on this earth, and I used her up. Those two things are both true. You can write that down or leave it out. I don’t much care which. But everything I love that’s near me ends up dyin’. Chasing the sun had cost me a wife and many kids. I knew right then, by being near to what I want to thrive, my curse would just hunt that down too.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the spite vow for the Boog name, defiant emotion, flat ('damnit' is generic not Hiberno)
lines.fixDirection: let the spite vow slip with an old tell rather than a generic oath
lines.span: I may be a curse, but damnit the Boog name will live on, purely out of spite of my detractors.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: telling Hollis not to be sorry, raw aftermath of the confession, flat
lines.fixDirection: re-Irish lightly so the aftermath keeps the old voice
lines.span: Don’t be. It’s an honest question. You’re the first person who ever asked it out loud.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: Mary always knew better, the emotional crux of the confession, flat American
lines.fixDirection: let the repeated 'Mary knew' beat surface one old tell
lines.span: Most people hear the story and they think I’m the hero. The immigrant who made it. The man who kept going. Mary knew better. Mary always knew better.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: pacing handoff to Hollis, flat baseline correct
lines.span: There is more to tell. Are you ready?

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: after Mary died, raising the children he feared ruining, grief memory, flat
lines.fixDirection: let the after-Mary memory slip with one phonetic tell
lines.span: After Mary died, I did what I always did. I worked. I bought land and kept the family fed. The children grew up around me and I tried not to ruin them the way I ruined her.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: wry recollection of the church-social woman, non-emotional, flat baseline correct
lines.span: Three years I went on like that. Then some woman at a church social decided I looked pitiful enough to need help.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: ghost-of-a-smile aside, light non-emotional baseline correct
lines.span: She was wrong about that. But I didn’t argue with who she introduced me to.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: correcting his age, flat baseline correct
lines.span: Fifty-eight.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: confirming then catching himself, flat baseline correct
lines.span: That’s right.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: bastard-child-in-Dublin memory and the old desertion wound, a prime slip beat, flat
lines.fixDirection: let the Dublin bastard-child memory slip into the old voice
lines.span: They weren’t the ones who were deserted. I was. I was a bastard child in Dublin, and I felt the same when I arrived here.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: framing Oregon as where something changed, reflective and off-guard, flat
lines.fixDirection: re-Irish lightly so the turning-point reflection keeps the old voice
lines.span: Oregon is a different story, Hollis. The other places, I arrived and I built. I arrived here and I built too. But something happened in this valley that didn’t happen in the other places.

lines.speaker: William
lines.note: the revelation that he stopped, quiet emotional climax, flat (the brevity here is control but the beat earns a tell)
lines.fixDirection: let one old tell surface on the 'I stopped' revelation
lines.span: I stopped.
